behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is module handoff: filesystem discovery + filename parsing/sorting + runner execution order. MOCK-MISS: a pure sort-function unit test can miss bugs where discovered files are passed unsorted to the executor. REAL-INFRA: no real OS/network/subprocess behavior beyond local filesystem is required. REJECTION-GATE: System was rejected because the behavior text only requires order from filename prefixes, not process/network/DB-engine behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp `migrations/` directory with out-of-order files (e.g., `010_b.sql`, `002_a.sql`, `100_c.sql`), run the runner with a spy/fake migration executor, and assert execution order is `002`, `010`, `100`.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: This is state handoff across modules (runner + `_migrations` metadata persistence) across two runs. Unit is too low because a single-function test can pass while persistence wiring is broken. REAL-INFRA does not require OS/subprocess concurrency; a local test DB is sufficient. REJECTION-GATE: System was considered but rejected because behavior text is only "subsequent run" skip logic, not real infra edge conditions like process races/timeouts.
behaviors.test_description: Run the runner twice against a local test DB with one migration that mutates data. Assert first run applies it and records it; second run does not execute the SQL again (data unchanged, no duplicate metadata row).

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends on real PostgreSQL transaction/error semantics plus runner control flow after a DB failure. MOCK-HIDE: stubs/fakes often cannot reproduce engine-accurate rollback behavior and failure propagation. REAL-INFRA: requires a real DB process connection. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was rejected because behavior text explicitly requires automatic rollback on statement failure, which is DB-engine behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Against a real PostgreSQL instance, provide migrations where migration N partially changes data then errors, and migration N+1 would make a detectable change. Assert N’s partial changes are absent after run (rolled back), N+1 was not attempted, and `_migrations` contains only prior successful versions.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: Failure mode is real concurrent-process race behavior with shared DB state/locking. MOCK-MISS and REAL-INFRA both force System: in-process fakes rarely expose true inter-process timing races. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was rejected because behavior text explicitly requires "two instances... start at the same time," which is subprocess/concurrency infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Launch two runner processes concurrently against the same real PostgreSQL DB and same migration set. Assert end state reflects single application of each migration and `_migrations` has one row per version (no duplicates) after both processes exit.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the `_migrations` table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: This checks startup orchestration order between metadata bootstrap and migration execution modules. Unit can miss ordering bugs across boundaries. REAL-INFRA: no subprocess race or OS primitive required; local DB wiring is enough. REJECTION-GATE: System was considered but rejected because behavior text is ordering on first run, not infra-specific failure modes (concurrency/network/subprocess behavior).
behaviors.test_description: Against a fresh test DB, run runner with one migration that depends on metadata tracking. Verify `_migrations` exists before first migration bookkeeping occurs (e.g., via query/log hook order or successful metadata insert tied to first migration) and run succeeds without missing-table errors.
